questions.scenario: AWS_DEPLOY_KEY is a repository secret. Only deploy-production references it today, but any future workflow job in the repository could add the same secrets reference. The team wants the value unavailable until a protected production deployment is approved. What should change?

options.text: Keep the repository secret, but restrict the workflow file with CODEOWNERS and branch protection.
Move the credential to an organization secret limited to the production repository.
Move the credential to the production environment and require the deploy job to target that protected environment.
Keep repository scope and add a manual approval job before the deployment job references the secret.

questions.explanation: Environment scope ties the secret to jobs that target production, and protection rules delay access until approval. CODEOWNERS protects reviewed changes but does not make the repository secret unavailable to another authorized workflow. Organization scope limits repositories, not jobs. A separate approval job also cannot enforce when a repository-scoped value becomes readable. See [GitHub Actions environments](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/configure-and-manage-deployments/manage-environments).

questions.scenario: A production job is queued with the message that one approval is required from payments-on-call. The workflow has reached the job, but no runner has begun deployment steps. Which control is working?

options.text: A production concurrency group is queuing the job behind an earlier deployment.
A required reviewer protection rule is withholding the environment until approval.
A deployment branch rule is waiting for the source ref to be evaluated.
An organization approval policy is holding the workflow run before any jobs are created.

questions.explanation: The named reviewer requirement is direct evidence that an environment protection rule is holding the deployment. Concurrency can also queue work, but it identifies another run rather than a reviewer. Branch rules reject or allow refs, and organization approval policies act at a different boundary. See [Reviewing deployments](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/configure-and-manage-deployments/review-deployments).

questions.scenario: A pull request test job only checks out source and runs unit tests. Its workflow-level permissions grant contents: write and packages: write because a later release job publishes images. The security standard also requires any future job to start read-only unless it explicitly declares a write need. Which review change satisfies both requirements?

options.text: Keep the workflow-level grants but override the test job to `contents: read` and `packages: read`.
Set the workflow default to read-only and use a GitHub App token with package write in every job.
Split publishing into a second workflow while leaving the current workflow-level write grants unchanged.
Set a read-only default and grant package write only on the publishing job.

questions.explanation: A read-only default plus a narrow publishing-job override makes least privilege visible and prevents future jobs from inheriting writes. Overriding only the current test job helps today but leaves broad rights as the default for new jobs. A GitHub App token can be appropriate for cross-repository work, but granting it everywhere repeats the same scope problem. Splitting workflows does not help if the broad permission remains. See [GITHUB_TOKEN authentication](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/authenticate-with-github_token).

questions.scenario: The production workflow uses aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials and no static AWS keys. The step fails before contacting AWS with a message that it cannot obtain an ID token. Which workflow permission is required?

options.text: id-token: write on the deployment job, along with only the other permissions that job needs.
actions: read on the deployment job so it can read the workflow's identity metadata.
contents: read and `AWS_ROLE_ARN` in the job environment, because the action derives an ID token automatically.
id-token: read at workflow level, because requesting a cloud token should not require write access.

questions.explanation: `id-token: write` authorizes the job to request a signed OIDC identity token; the unusual verb does not grant repository write access. Repository or action read permissions do not authorize token minting, and `id-token: read` is not a supported substitute. AWS then validates the token and can issue temporary credentials. See [GitHub OIDC reference](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/oidc).

questions.scenario: An AWS role trust policy allows only the subject repo:acme/checkout-api:environment:production. A workflow from the same repository runs on main but does not target the production environment. Can it assume the role?

options.text: Yes, if branch protection allows only reviewed changes to reach `main`.
Yes, if the workflow declares `environment: production` on a later step rather than on the job.
No, because its subject claim does not contain the required production environment context.
Yes, because the role permission policy is evaluated after the repository portion of the subject matches.

questions.explanation: The trust policy compares the exact token claims with its conditions. A main-branch subject and an environment subject are different identities, and the environment must be declared at job scope to affect the token subject. Adding the main subject would change the policy, but under the stated condition the current job is denied. See [Configuring OIDC in AWS](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/security-harden-deployments/oidc-in-aws).

questions.scenario: A customer-facing service is replacing repository AWS keys with a protected, auditable deployment flow. Which controls form a safer production path from GitHub Actions?

options.text: A repository-level OIDC role shared by staging and production with branch-only subject restrictions.
A protected production environment with reviewers and deployment branch rules.
OIDC trust restricted to the intended repository and production environment.
One production role for every repository, restricted by organization name but not repository or environment.
A short-lived role whose permissions cover only the required production resources and actions.

questions.explanation: Environment protection controls when the job runs, OIDC conditions control which workflow identity may assume the role, and least-privilege permissions control what the session can do. A branch-only shared role does not distinguish staging from production. An organization-wide role leaves too many repositories eligible even though its credentials are temporary. See [GitHub OIDC](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/openid-connect).
